content.problemStatement:
There are **N** students living in the dormitory of Berland State University. Each of them sometimes wants to use the kitchen, so the head of the dormitory came up with a timetable for kitchen's usage in order to avoid the conflicts:



The first student starts to use the kitchen at the time **0** and should finish the cooking not later than at the time **A1**.
The second student starts to use the kitchen at the time **A1** and should finish the cooking not later than at the time **A2**.
And so on.
The **N**-th student starts to use the kitchen at the time **AN-1** and should finish the cooking not later than at the time **AN**
The holidays in Berland are approaching, so today each of these **N** students wants to cook some pancakes. The **i**-th student needs **Bi** units of time to cook.

The students have understood that probably not all of them will be able to cook everything they want. How many students will be able to cook without violating the schedule?


Input Format
The first line of the input contains an integer **T** denoting the number of test cases. The description of **T** test cases follows. 

Each test case contains 3 lines of input



The first line of each test case contains a single integer **N** denoting the number of students.
The second line contains **N** space-separated integers **A1**, **A2**, ..., **AN** denoting the moments of time by when the corresponding student should finish cooking.
The third line contains **N** space-separated integers **B1**, **B2**, ..., **BN** denoting the time required for each of the students to cook.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing the number of students that will be able to finish the cooking.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case contains 3 lines of input: the first line contains a single integer N denoting the number of students, the second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, ..., AN denoting the moments of time by when the corresponding student should finish cooking, and the third line contains N space-separated integers B1, B2, ..., BN denoting the time required for each of the students to cook.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of students that will be able to finish the cooking.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, the first student has 1 unit of time and needs 1 unit of time, so they can cook. The second student has 9 units of time and needs 10 units of time, so they cannot cook. The third student has 5 units of time and needs 3 units of time, so they can cook. In the second test case, the first student has 10 units of time and needs 15 units of time, so they cannot cook. The second student has 10 units of time and needs 5 units of time, so they can cook. The third student has 10 units of time and needs 20 units of time, so they cannot cook.

solution.approach: The approach is to iterate over each student and check if they have enough time to cook. We start with the first student who has A1 units of time to cook and needs B1 units of time. If B1 <= A1, the student can cook and we move to the next student who starts at time A1 and needs B2 units of time. If at any point a student cannot cook (B1 > A1), we break the loop and output the number of students who were able to cook.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of students in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) as we only use a constant amount of space to store the current student's cooking time and the number of students who can cook.

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    B = list(map(int, input().split()))
    count = 0
    time = 0
    for i in range(N):
        if time + B[i] <= A[i]:
            count += 1
            time = A[i]
    print(count)
